channel.title: NYT > Arts > Music
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Zen of Peter Frampton
item.description: Grappling with a progressive muscular disease and making a new album with his son, the onetime British guitar hero may be happier than ever.
item.dc:creator: Grayson Haver Currin and Eric Ryan Anderson
item.media:credit: Eric Ryan Anderson for The New York Times

item.title: Streisand, LuPone, Holliday, Menzel: Broadway’s Biggest Voices
item.description: Lea Michele, Christine Ebersole, Jennifer Hudson, Kristin Chenoweth, Adam Lambert and 10 others reflect on their favorite belters.

item.title: Britney Spears Pleads Guilty to Reckless Driving After D.U.I. Charge
item.description: As part of a plea deal, the pop star will serve 12 months probation, complete a three-month substance abuse program and pay a modest fine.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Esmé E. Deprez
item.media:credit: Danny Moloshok/Reuters
item.media:description: A representative for Britney Spears said after her arrest that her actions were “completely inexcusable.”

item.title: Ashley McBryde Saved Her Own Life. Now She’s Rocking Out Her Way.
item.description: The prolific country singer and songwriter has never quite fit the country music mold. Her upcoming album, “Wild,” revels in her individuality, and her sobriety.
item.dc:creator: David Peisner and Meghan Marin
item.media:description: “What made me have to stop drinking was the possibility of self-harm,” McBryde said. “After losing my brother to suicide, I was so scared that that’s somehow genetic.”

item.title: Kennedy Center Ticketing Union Files Labor Charge Over Layoffs
item.description: The International Alliance of Theatrical Stage Employees challenged the terminations in a complaint to the National Labor Relations Board.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: International Alliance of Theatrical Stage Employees
Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/Associated Press
item.media:description: The union representing ticketing staff is challenging the layoff of its members at the Kennedy Center, which is preparing to shut down for two years, starting in July.

item.title: Gabriela Lena Frank’s ‘Picaflor’ Wins Pulitzer for Music
item.description: Gabriela Lena Frank’s “Picaflor: A Future Myth,” premiered by the Philadelphia Orchestra, has won the prestigious award for music.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Carlos Chavarria for The New York Times
item.media:description: The composer Gabriela Lena Frank, whose work “Picaflor: A Future Myth” won the Pulitzer Prize for music.

item.title: Chasing Stardom in Korea Nearly Destroyed Ejae. Then Came ‘KPop Demon Hunters.’
item.description: Ejae, the movie’s breakout star, was the face of K-pop in 2025. Facing questions of art, national belonging and authenticity, she wants to be much more.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:credit: Woohae Cho for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ejae in the Hannam neighborhood of Seoul in April. She spent about a decade training to be a K-pop star in the city.

item.title: Seymour Bernstein Dies at 99; Stage Fright Cut Short His Piano Career
item.description: After giving his last public concert in 1977, he moved into teaching. A 2014 documentary directed by Ethan Hawke put him in the spotlight once more.
item.dc:creator: Vivien Schweitzer
item.media:credit: Emon Hassan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Seymour Bernstein at his home on the Upper West Side of Manhattan in 2014. “I hated the commercial aspect,” he said of his performing career. “I hated the nerves.”

item.title: Andy Kershaw, Radio D.J. Who Tuned Britons Into World Music, Dies at 66
item.description: A longtime BBC host, he traveled the world in search of artists his audience “didn’t know they wanted.” Then he took an unexpected turn into war reporting.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Steve Pyke/Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Kershaw in 1996. After he soured on 1980s British bands, he began traveling to other countries to find new music for his listeners.

item.title: 16 Photos That Captured the Dawn of Punk
item.description: In the 1970s and ’80s, Stephanie Chernikowski, who has died at 84, shot bands like the Ramones and Blondie at CBGB and other downtown clubs.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams and Stephanie Chernikowski

item.title: Stephanie Chernikowski, 84, Dies; Photographed the ‘Rough Magic’ of Punk
item.description: She captured the radiant chaos at New York City clubs like CBGB in the 1970s, when bands like the Ramones, Blondie and the Patti Smith Group were emerging.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: At the Kennedy Center, the National Symphony’s Show Goes On
item.description: With ambition that exceeded its grasp, the orchestra performed Puccini’s “Il Trittico” ahead of the center’s expected closure.
item.dc:creator: Oussama Zahr
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: The Taylor Swift Interview: Behind the Scenes
item.description: On “Popcast,” the reporter Joe Coscarelli discusses what it was like to talk to Swift about songwriting with the critic Jon Caramanica.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica, Joe Coscarelli and Kate LoPresti
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Kacey Musgraves Sounds Right at Home in the ‘Middle of Nowhere’
item.description: The country singer and songwriter’s sixth album is a triumph of self-realization that reunites her with early collaborators and a former foe (Miranda Lambert).
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Jason Kempin/Getty Images
item.media:description: Kacey Musgraves’s new album is a meditation on the differences between the ache of loneliness and the contentment of solitude.

item.title: Asmik Grigorian on Performing the Opera ‘Eugene Onegin’
item.description: The soprano Asmik Grigorian is starring as Tatiana, one of her signature roles, in Tchaikovsky’s classic at the Metropolitan Opera.
item.dc:creator: Simon Morrison
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: The soprano Asmik Grigorian, who is singing in “Eugene Onegin” at the Metropolitan Opera through May 16.

item.title: Sofiane Pamart Emerged From France’s Rap Scene. Now, He’s Setting the Piano World on Fire.
item.description: Sofiane Pamart is on a mission to make his instrument as popular, and spectacular, as possible.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sofiane Pamart in Paris. “I want my piano to be unifying and to create bonds between all generations, all social classes,” he said.

item.title: ‘Moby Dick’ Review: Robert Wilson’s Last Masterpiece
item.description: Wilson’s 2024 adaptation of Herman Melville’s classic, with music by the British singer-songwriter Anna Calvi, has a short run at the Brooklyn Academy of Music.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Christopher Nell, left, as the sublime Boy and Rosa Enskat as Captain Ahab in Robert Wilson’s “Moby Dick” at the Brooklyn Academy of Music.

item.title: Britney Spears Is Charged With D.U.I. Involving Drugs and Alcohol
item.description: Officers in California observed the pop star driving fast and erratically in March. After she was arrested, she checked herself into a treatment facility.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Eduardo Munoz/Reuters
item.media:description: Britney Spears was in a conservatorship for 13 years because of what her father said were public mental health struggles and possible substance abuse.

item.title: Fortnite Issues Refunds for D4vd Gameplay Items
item.description: The singer, who has been charged with the murder of 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez, began his career making music inspired by the game.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Ted Soqui - Pool/Getty Images
item.media:description: David Burke has pleaded not guilty to a first-degree murder charge in connection with the killing of Celeste Rivas Hernandez.

item.title: David Allan Coe, Singer Who Personified Outlaw Country, Dies at 86
item.description: Mr. Coe, who wrote “Take This Job and Shove It” and other hits, was a transgressive exponent of the outlaw country movement of the 1970s and ’80s.
item.dc:creator: Bill Friskics-Warren
item.media:credit: Michael Ochs Archives/Getty Images
item.media:description: David Allan Coe around 1970. From his debut, he registered as an original with an offbeat sense of humor, a deep baritone and a fierce resolve to be different.

item.title: Rush Lost Its Beloved Drummer. The Band Is Ready to Get Back on the Road.
item.description: After losing Neil Peart to cancer six years ago, the trio is returning to touring with Anika Nilles, a powerhouse drummer who’s forged her own relationship with the group.
item.dc:creator: Hank Shteamer
item.media:credit: Nathan Cyprys for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left: Geddy Lee, Anika Nilles, Alex Lifeson and Loren Gold. The new touring lineup of the band Rush will hit the road in June.

item.title: D4vd Bought Chain Saws to Dispose of Celeste Rivas Hernandez’s Body, Prosecutors Say
item.description: In a legal filing, prosecutors outlined for the first time how they say the singer murdered and dismembered Celeste Rivas Hernandez.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens, Emmanuel Morgan and Orlando Mayorquín
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A neighbor of Celeste Rivas Hernandez’s at a memorial in Lake Elsinore, Calf.

item.title: Tupac Shakur’s Stepbrother Files Wrongful Death Lawsuit, 29 Years After Murder
item.description: Maurice Shakur said in a complaint filed against Duane Keith Davis, who was charged in 2023 with the rapper’s murder, that the “threads are starting to come together.”
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: The rapper Tupac Shakur was shot four times in a drive-by shooting in Las Vegas in 1996. He died days later at the age of 25.

item.title: ‘Evita,’ Starring Rachel Zegler, Is Heading to Broadway
item.description: A buzzy revival of Andrew Lloyd Webber’s hit 1970s musical will transfer to New York next spring, but without its signature outdoor scene.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Marc Brenner
item.media:description: Rachel Zegler won an Olivier Award this month for her performance in “Evita” in London. Now she plans to return to the production when it heads to Broadway.

item.title: The New York Philharmonic Gives Gustavo Dudamel a Warm Welcome
item.description: The flamboyant conductor Gustavo Dudamel got a jubilant reception at the New York Philharmonic’s spring gala on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr and Dolly Faibyshev
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Dolly Faibyshev for The New York Times

item.title: Takeaways From The Times’s Taylor Swift Interview on ‘Our Song,’ ‘All Too Well’ and More
item.description: Swift spoke with The New York Times about confessional songwriting, the inspiration behind some of her biggest hits and a writing quirk she can’t stand.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin

item.title: Kennedy Center Works to Make the Case That Repairs Are Urgent
item.description: In Washington and in federal court, the center is arguing that its planned two-year closure is crucial. Critics say it’s a result of declining attendance and fleeing artists.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Zach Montague
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Among the changes the Trump administration has brought to the Kennedy Center is the addition of the president’s name to the exterior. Now it is slated to close for reasons that are the subject of debate.

item.title: 11 Great American Songwriters Who Didn’t Make Our List
item.description: The New York Times Magazine named the 30 Greatest Living American Songwriters. Here are more contenders, including Billy Joel, Frank Ocean and Neko Case.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.category: Hurray for the Riff Raff (Music Group)
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: Billy Joel

item.title: Geirr Tveitt’s Music Captured Norway’s Colors. His Life Reflected Its Shadows.
item.description: Geirr Tveitt’s reputation was tarnished by his nationalist politics. But a new generation of musicians is bringing this composer’s music to an international audience.
item.dc:creator: Corinna da Fonseca-Wollheim
item.media:credit: Aage Storløkken/Alamy
item.media:description: The Norwegian composer Geirr Tveitt in 1973, a few years after he lost about 80 percent of his compositions in a fire.

item.title: ‘Flower Drum Song,’ Once Overhauled, Returns Changed Again
item.description: David Henry Hwang revised the Rodgers & Hammerstein classic 25 years ago. Now he gets to remedy some of his own remake’s flaws.
item.dc:creator: Robert Ito
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Henry Hwang’s revival of Rodgers and Hammerstein’s “Flower Drum Song” has returned to Los Angeles 25 years after it first premiered.

item.title: She’s Blazing a Trail for a Traditional Korean Zither in Jazz
item.description: The Seoul-born gayageum player DoYeon Kim has become an in-demand collaborator for some of the biggest innovators in improvised music.
item.dc:creator: Shaun Brady and Vincent Alban
item.media:description: “I need to figure out why I was born in this time, in this place, and why I chose music.” Kim said. “There must be a reason and a way I can use it to serve the world.”

item.title: The Obamas, Kim Kardashian and Other A-Listers Are Now Broadway Co-Producers
item.description: The latest trend on Broadway is celebrity co-producing: A-listers who now have credits as backers of plays and musicals.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Illustration by Max-o-matic. Photographs via Pexels/Loquellano (street sign); Unsplash/Barry Weatherall (curtain); Getty Images

item.title: Jay-Z Breaks Down the Stories Behind His Most Famous Songs
item.description: The artist shares stories behind his famous songs, the writing challenges he sets for himself and how he has sustained a hip-hop career.

item.title: How Taylor Swift Writes a Song, In Her Own Words
item.description: The artist shares stories behind some of her biggest hits, her love of a “rant bridge” and how life in the public eye informs the stories she tells in her songs.
